title: My Dog Ate Fertilizer Pellets: Toxicity Guide & Triage Steps
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your dog ate fertilizer pellets. Discover urgency levels, symptoms to watch for, and how a photo of the bag helps your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Fertilizer ingestion is typically a medium-level emergency that requires immediate veterinary consultation; having a photo of the product packaging is essential for determining the risk level.

What happens if my dog eats fertilizer?

Fertilizer ingestion occurs when a dog consumes plant food pellets, which typically contain a concentrated mix of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (labeled as N-P-K). While many standard lawn fertilizers are formulated to be relatively safe once spread, the concentrated pellets can contain additives such as iron, herbicides, pesticides, or organic meals that pose significant health risks when swallowed in bulk.

Is it an emergency if my dog ate fertilizer?

  • The urgency level for fertilizer ingestion is generally Medium.
  • While most exposures result in mild gastrointestinal upset like vomiting or diarrhea, the situation can escalate quickly depending on the ingredients.
  • Fertilizers containing high levels of iron can lead to iron toxicity.
  • Organic fertilizers (like those containing blood or bone meal) can cause severe pancreatitis or even form a solid mass in the stomach.
  • You should contact a veterinarian immediately to assess the specific risk based on your dog's weight and the amount consumed.

How does taking a photo of the packaging help my vet?

  • If you are calling a vet or an emergency clinic, having a clear photo of the fertilizer packaging is one of the most helpful tools you can provide.
  • Different brands use vastly different chemical concentrations.
  • A photo of the ingredient list and the N-P-K ratio allows the veterinary team to calculate exactly how much of each toxin your dog ingested.
  • This information helps them determine whether you need to come in for emergency treatment or if you can monitor your pet at home.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of fertilizer pellets by a dog, prompt decontamination is warranted, especially with elemental iron ingestion exceeding 20 mg/kg; emesis should be induced, but activated charcoal is not effective for iron binding. For ingestions greater than 60 mg/kg, additional treatment and monitoring are necessary. Milk of magnesia (5-30 mL once or twice daily) can be administered to decrease iron absorption. Serum iron levels and total serum iron binding capacity should be checked at 3 and 8-10 hours post-exposure. Chelation therapy should be considered if serum iron is >300 mcg/dL with clinical signs such as repeated vomiting and shock, or if it exceeds the total iron binding capacity. Be aware that oliguria and anuria secondary to shock-induced renal failure may also occur.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of fertilizer poisoning?
faqs.answer: Symptoms often include drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and a bloated appearance. In severe cases involving iron or pesticides, you may see muscle tremors, seizures, or dark-colored gums.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian or a pet poison control expert. Depending on the ingredients, inducing vomiting could cause further damage to the esophagus or lead to aspiration.

faqs.question: Is organic fertilizer safer for dogs?
faqs.answer: Actually, organic fertilizers can be more dangerous because they often contain bone meal, blood meal, or fish meal. These ingredients are very tasty to dogs, leading them to eat large quantities, which can cause severe pancreatitis or gastric obstructions.
